## STOCHASTIC INVERSE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The goal of the inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) problem is to recover the reward functions from expert demonstrations. However, the IRL problem like any ill-posed inverse problem suffers the congenital defect that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions, and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions. We adopt the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) method to estimate the parameter of the probability distribution as the first solution to the SIRL problem. The solution is _succinct, robust, and transferable_ for a learning task and can generate alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Through our formulation, it is possible to observe the intrinsic property for the IRL problem from a _global_ viewpoint, and our approach achieves a considerable performance on the _objectworld_ .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The IRL problem addresses an inverse problem that a set of expert demonstrations determines a reward function over a Markov decision process (MDP) if the model dynamics are known Russell (1998); Ng et al. (2000). The recovered reward function provides a _succinct, robust, and transferable_ definition of the learning task and completely determines the optimal policy. However, the IRL problem is ill-posed that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. For example, all policies are optimal for a constant reward function. In a real-world scenario, experts always act sub-optimally or inconsistently, which is another challenge.

To overcome these limitations, two classes of probabilistic approaches for the IRL problem are proposed, i.e., Bayesian inverse reinforcement learning (BIRL) Ramachandran & Amir (2007) based on Bayesians’ maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation and maximum entropy IRL (MaxEnt) Ziebart et al. (2008); Ziebart (2010) based on frequentists’ maximum likelihood (MLE) estimation. BIRL solves for the distribution of reward functions without an assumption that experts behave optimally, and encode the external _a priori_ information in a choice of a prior distribution. However, BIRL also suffers from the practical limitation that a large number of algorithmic iterations is required for the procedure of Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) in a sampling of posterior over reward functions. Advanced techniques, for example Kernel technique Michini & How (2012) and gradient method Choi & Kim (2011), are proposed to improve the efficiency and tractability of this situation.

MaxEnt employs the principle of maximum entropy to resolve the ambiguity in choosing demonstrations over a policy. This class of methods, inheriting the merits from previous non-probabilistic IRL approaches including Ng et al. (2000); Abbeel & Ng (2004); Ratliff et al. (2006); Abbeel et al. (2008); Syed & Schapire (2008); Ho et al. (2016), imposes regular structures of reward functions in a combination of hand-selected features. Formally, the reward function is a linear or nonlinear combination of the feature basis functions which consists of a set of real-valued functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}i_ =1 hand-selected by experts. The goal of this approach is to find the best-fitting weights of feature basis functions through the MLE approach. Wulfmeier et al. (2015) and Levine et al. (2011) use deep neural networks and Gaussian processes to fit the parameters based on demonstrations respectively but still suffer from the problem that the true reward shaped by the changing environment dynamics.

Influenced by the work of Finn et al. (2016a;b), Fu et al. (2017) propose a framework called adversarial IRL (AIRL) to recover robust reward functions in a changing dynamics based on adversarial learning and achieves superior results. Compared with AIRL, another adversarial method called generative

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) Ho & Ermon (2016) seeks to directly recover the expert’s policy rather than reward functions. Many follow-up methods enhance and extend GAIL for multipurpose in various application scenarios Li et al. (2017); Hausman et al. (2017); Wang et al. (2017). However, GAIL is in a lack of an explanation of expert’s behavior and a portable representation for the knowledge transfer which are the merits of the class of the MaxEnt approach, because the MaxEnt approach is equipped with the "transferable" regular structures over reward functions.

In this paper, under the framework of the MaxEnt approach, we propose a generalized perspective of studying the IRL problem called _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL). It is formulated as an expectation optimization problem aiming to recover a probability distribution over the reward function from expert demonstrations. The solution of SIRL is _succinct_ and _robust_ for the learning task in the meaning that it can generate more than one weight over feature basis functions which compose alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Benefits of the class of the MaxEnt method, the solution to our generalized problem SIRL is also _transferable_ . Since of the intractable integration in our formulation, we employ the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) approach Wei & Tanner (1990) to give the first solution to the SIRL problem in a model-based environment. In general, the solutions to the IRL problem are not always best-fitting in the previous approaches because a highly nonlinear inverse problem with the limited information is very likely to get trapped in a secondary maximum in the recovery. Taking advantage of the Monte Carlo mechanism of a _global_ exhaustive search, our MCEM approach avoids the secondary maximum and theoretically convergent demonstrated by pieces of literature Caffo et al. (2005); Chan & Ledolter (1995). Our approach is also quickly convergent because of the preset simple geometric configuration over weight space in which we approximate it with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). Hence, our approach works well in a real-world scenario with a small and variability set of expert demonstrations.

In particular, the contributions of this paper are threefold:

1. We generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem SIRL.

2. We provide the first theoretically existing solution to SIRL by the MCEM approach.

3. We show the effectiveness of our approach by comparing the performance of the proposed method to those of the previous algorithms on the _objectworld_ .

## 2 PRELIMINARY

An MDP is a tuple _M_ := _⟨S, A, T , R, γ⟩_ , where _S_ is the set of states, _A_ is the set of actions, and the transition function (a.k.a. model dynamics) _T_ := P( _st_ +1 = _s[′]_[��] _st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) for _s, s[′] ∈S_ and _a ∈A_ is the probability of being current state _s_ , taking action _a_ and yielding next state _s[′]_ . Reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) is a real-valued function and _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) is the discount factor. A policy _π_ : _S →A_ is deterministic or stochastic, where the deterministic one is written as _a_ = _π_ ( _s_ ), and the stochastic one is as a conditional distribution _π_ ( _a|s_ ). Sequential decisions are recorded in a series of episodes which consist of states _s_ , actions _a_ , and rewards _r_ . The goal of reinforcement learning aims to get optimal policy _π[∗]_ for maximizing the expected total reward, i.e.

Given an MDP without a reward function _R_ , i.e., MDP _\_ R= _⟨S, A, T ⟩_ , and _m_ expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ := _{ζ_[1] _, · · · , ζ[m] }_ . Each expert demonstration _ζ[i]_ is a sequential of state-action pairs. The goal of the IRL problem is to estimate the unknown reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) from expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ and the reward functions compose a complete MDP. The estimated complete MDP yields an optimal policy that acts as closely as the expert demonstrations.

## 2.2.1 NOTE:

- Problem 1 is well-posed and typically not intractable analytically.

- Trajectory element set _O_ is usually known from the rough estimation of the statistics in expert demonstrations in practice.

- We introduce _representative trajectory class_ to overcome the limitation of the sub-optimality of expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ Ramachandran & Amir (2007), which is also called a lack of sampling representativeness in statistics Kruskal & Mosteller (1979), e.g., driver’s demonstrations encode his own preferences in driving style but may not reflect the true rewards of an environment. With the technique of representative trajectory class, each subset of driver’s demonstrations (i.e. trajectory element set _O_ ) constitutes a subproblem in Problem 1.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate our approach on a classic environment _objectworld_ introduced by Levine et al. (2011) which is a particularly challenging environment with a large number of irrelevant features and the highly nonlinearity of the reward functions. Note that since almost only _objectworld_ provides a tool that allows analysis and display the evolution procedure of the SIRL problem in a 2D heat map, we skip the typical invisible physics-based control tasks for the evaluation of our approach, i.e. cartpole Barto et al. (1983), mountain car Moore (1990), MuJoCo Todorov et al. (2012), and etc.
We employ the expected value difference (EVD) proposed by Levine et al. (2011) to be the metric of optimality as follows:

which is a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy _π[∗]_ , given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ), where Θ _[∗]_ is the best estimation parameter in our approach.

## 4.1 OBJECTWORLD

The _objectworld_ is a learning environment for the IRL problem. It is an _N × N_ grid board with colored objects placed in randomly selected cells. Each colored object is assigned one inner color and one outer color from _C_ preselected colors. Each cell on the grid board is a state, and stepping to four neighbor cells (up, down, left, right) or staying in place (stay) are five actions with a 30% chance of moving in a random direction.

The ground truth of the reward function is defined in the following way. Suppose two primary colors of _C_ preselected colors are red and blue. The reward of a state is 1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object and 2 steps of an outer blue object, -1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object, and 0 otherwise. The other pairs of inner and outer colors are distractors. Continuous and discrete versions of feature basis functions are provided. For the continuous version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a 2 _C_ -dimensional real-valued feature vector. Each dimension records the Euclidean distance from the state to objects. For example, the first and second coordinates are the distances to the nearest inner and outer red object respectively, and so on through all _C_ colors. For the discrete version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a (2 _C · N_ )-dimensional binary feature vector. Each _N_ -dimensional vector records a binary representation of the distance to the nearest inner or outer color object with the _d_ -th coordinate 1 if the corresponding continuous distance is less than _d_ .

Additionally, the architecture of neural networks in DeepMaxEnt and DSIRL are implemented as 3-layer fully-connected with the sigmoid function.

## 4.2.1 EVALUATION PLATFORM

All the methods are implemented in Python 3.5 and Theano 1.0.0 with a machine learning distributed framework _Ray_ Moritz et al. (2018). The experiments are conducted on a machine with Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7700 CPU @ 3.60GHz and Nvidia GeForce GTX 1070 GPU.

## 4.2.2 RECOVERY EXPERIMENT

In the recovery experiment, we compare the true reward function, the optimal policy, and the optimal value with ones derived from expert demonstrations under the four methods. Since our approach is an average of all the outcomes which are prone to imitate the true optimal value from expert demonstrations, we use the mean of reward conditional probability distribution for SIRL and DSIRL as a comparison.

In Figure 1, the EVD of the optimal values in the last row are 48.9, 31.1, 33.7 and 11.3 for four methods respectively, and the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively. It yields that in a highly nonlinear inverse problem, the recovery abilities of SIRL and DISRL are better than MaxEnt’s and DeepMaxEnt’s respectively. The reason is mainly because Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach alleviates the problem of getting stuck in local minima by allowing random exit from it.

## 4.2.4 HYPERPARAMETER EXPERIMENT

In the hyperparameter experiment, we evaluate the effectiveness of our approach under the influence of different preset quantities and qualities of expert demonstrations. The amount of information carried in expert demonstrations composes a specific learning environment, and hence it has an impact on the effectiveness of our generative model. We verify three hyperparameters including the number of expert demonstrations in Figure 3, the trajectory length of expert demonstrations in Figure 4 and the portion size in representative trajectory class _Cϵ[E]_[in Figure][ 5][ on the] _[ objectworld]_[.][The] shadow of the line in the figures represents the standard error for each experimental trail. Notice that the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing as the number and the trajectory length of expert demonstrations, and the portion size in the representative trajectory class are increasing. A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose a generalized problem SIRL for the IRL problem to get the distribution of reward functions. The new problem is well-posed and we employ the method of MCEM to give the first _succinct, robust, and transferable_ solution. In the experiment, we evaluate our approach on the _objectworld_ and the experimental results confirm the effectiveness of our approach.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), a framework that treats the IRL problem as an expectation optimization task to recover a distribution over reward functions. By employing a Monte Carlo Expectation-Maximization (MCEM) approach, the authors aim to address the inherent non-identifiability of reward functions from expert demonstrations. The method is evaluated on the Objectworld environment, showing improvements over standard MaxEnt and DeepMaxEnt baselines.

The paper addresses a fundamental challenge in IRL by moving from point estimation to a probabilistic distribution of potential rewards. While the formulation is conceptually interesting, the lack of theoretical convergence analysis and the reliance on a specific, small-scale grid environment limit the impact of the findings. The paper needs more rigorous evidence that the proposed stochastic approach scales and provides tangible benefits beyond simple ambiguity reduction.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Convergence Guarantees: Section 3.3 discusses the convergence of the MCEM algorithm but fails to provide a formal proof or a clear statement of the conditions required for the algorithm to reach a stationary point. The paper references literature on MCEM convergence (Caffo et al., 2005; Chan & Ledolter, 1995) but does not demonstrate that these conditions hold for the specific objective function defined in Equation 1. Given that the reward recovery is highly nonlinear, the absence of a proof or at least a discussion of the regularity conditions on the reward feature space makes the claims about the algorithm's global search capability difficult to verify. Providing a sketch of the convergence proof or specifying the necessary assumptions on the GMM approximation would significantly strengthen the theoretical contribution.

- Ambiguity in 'Representative Trajectory Class': The definition of the 'representative trajectory class' in Section 2.2 and the use of the threshold parameter epsilon (Equation 1) are under-specified. It is unclear how the choice of epsilon impacts the resulting distribution of reward functions, and there is no sensitivity analysis provided to justify the value of 0.95 used in the experiments. If the results are highly sensitive to this hyperparameter, the method's robustness is questionable. The authors should provide an ablation study showing how varying epsilon affects the EVD metric to clarify whether this threshold is a tuning knob or a fundamental component of the model.

- Limited Scope of Empirical Evaluation: The paper relies exclusively on the Objectworld environment for evaluation. While Objectworld is a standard benchmark for testing reward nonlinearity, it is a relatively simple grid-based task that does not capture the complexities of modern IRL applications, such as high-dimensional continuous control or visual state spaces. The authors claim the method is 'succinct, robust, and transferable,' but these claims are not supported by experiments in more complex environments or tasks involving transfer learning. To support the claim of transferability, the paper should include at least one experiment where a reward distribution learned in one environment is applied to a related, but distinct, task.

- Computational Scalability Concerns: The MCEM approach requires multiple inner-loop policy optimizations for each sample in the reward weight space (Section 3.1.2). As the state space grows or the reward function becomes more complex, the cost of these optimizations will likely become prohibitive. The paper does not provide a complexity analysis or a comparison of the computational time relative to adversarial methods like GAIL or AIRL. Without an assessment of how the number of samples (N_t) and the inner-loop optimization steps (m) scale with the dimensionality of the state space, it is difficult to assess the practical utility of SIRL for real-world applications.

- Missing concrete verification of the 'transferable' property: The authors frequently claim their solution is 'transferable,' yet the experimental section provides no evidence of this. To substantiate this claim, the paper needs a transfer learning experiment where a reward distribution learned in one version of the Objectworld (e.g., with a specific object configuration) is used to guide policy learning in a modified version of the environment. Without demonstrating that the recovered distribution $\mathcal{D}^{\mathcal{R}}(\mathcal{W}|\zeta^{E}, \Theta^{*})$ actually improves performance or sample efficiency in a target task, the claim of transferability remains speculative.

- Lack of a non-vacuous example for the 'representative trajectory class': The definition of the 'representative trajectory class' $\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}$ is central to the proposed SIRL formulation, yet it is presented abstractly. The paper would benefit from a worked example using a simple 2D Gridworld where the expert demonstrations are intentionally sub-optimal or inconsistent. Specifically, the authors should show how the set $\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}$ filters or weights these demonstrations to produce a more robust reward distribution compared to standard MaxEnt IRL. This would clarify whether the mechanism effectively handles the 'congenital defect' of IRL as claimed.

- Absence of computational complexity analysis: The paper acknowledges that the MCEM approach involves nested loops of policy optimization, but it lacks a formal or empirical discussion of computational scaling. To make the paper complete, the authors should include a complexity analysis relative to the number of samples $N_t$ and the inner-loop optimization steps $m$. Additionally, providing a wall-clock time comparison against a baseline like GAIL on the Objectworld environment would allow readers to gauge the practical overhead of the 'global exhaustive search' mentioned in the introduction.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more rigorous theoretical discussion or proof sketch regarding the convergence of the MCEM procedure under the specified conditions.

- Include an ablation study on the hyperparameter epsilon to demonstrate the sensitivity of the reward recovery to the choice of the representative trajectory class.

- Add at least one experiment in a continuous control or more complex environment to substantiate the claims of robustness and transferability.

- Include a computational complexity analysis comparing SIRL to baseline methods like AIRL or MaxEnt IRL.

comments.quote:
$\Theta^{*}:=\arg\max_{\Theta}\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{O}\in\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}}\big{[}\int_{\mathcal{W}}f_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{W}|\Theta)d\mathcal{W}\big{]},$ (1)

where trajec
comments.explanation: The objective function requires an expectation over the power set of expert demonstrations, which is combinatorial in size. For any non-trivial epsilon, this sum is exponential in the number of demonstrations, making the objective function computationally intractable. The paper does not define a sampling strategy or variational approximation to bypass this summation. Please rewrite the objective to explicitly define the sampling mechanism used to make this expectation tractable.

In standard MDP formulations, the transition dynamics cannot be fully evaluated for the final action if the terminal state is excluded. coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), which models the reward function as a distribution over feature weights estimated via a Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) algorithm. The authors claim this approach resolves the ill-posed nature of standard IRL by capturing solution multiplicity and avoiding local minima. While the probabilistic framing is conceptually sound, the algorithmic derivation contains significant notational and procedural ambiguities, and the experimental validation is confined to a single grid-world environment without standard statistical reporting or reward recovery metrics.

The paper tackles a well-known challenge in inverse reinforcement learning by attempting to recover a distribution over reward weights rather than a single point estimate. The idea of using a Gaussian mixture model to capture reward ambiguity is relevant to current research in probabilistic IRL. The empirical results on the Objectworld environment show that the method can produce diverse policies with low value gaps. However, the mathematical formulation of the optimization routine is unclear, the convergence claims rely on loosely connected literature, and the evaluation lacks the rigor expected for a top-tier venue.

Overview issues:

- Algorithmic derivation conflates latent sampling with parameter updates: Equation 1 establishes an expectation maximization objective over a joint density of trajectories and reward weights. Sections 3.1.2 and 3.1.3 then describe a two-stage update where the first stage optimizes reward weights via gradient ascent, and the second stage fits a GMM to those weights. This procedure breaks the standard EM decomposition. In a proper MCEM algorithm, the E-step computes expectations over the posterior of latent variables, while the M-step maximizes the expected complete-data log-likelihood. Directly optimizing the latent weights for each Monte Carlo sample destroys the monotonicity guarantees of EM. The authors must either reformulate the updates to strictly follow the E-step/M-step structure or provide a rigorous derivation showing why this variant converges to a stationary point.

- Convergence claims lack theoretical grounding for the IRL setting: The authors assert that the proposed method is theoretically convergent by citing Caffo et al. (2005) and Chan & Ledolter (1995). These references establish convergence for curved exponential families and time-series count models, respectively. Neither paper addresses reward function recovery in Markov decision processes or the specific non-convex landscape of IRL. The condition sum(1/N_t) < infinity is a standard stochastic approximation requirement, but it does not guarantee convergence to a global optimum or even a local minimum in this high-dimensional setting. The manuscript should either provide a convergence proof tailored to their specific objective function or tone down the theoretical claims and focus on empirical behavior.

- Experimental scope is restricted to a single toy environment: The evaluation relies exclusively on the Objectworld grid environment because the authors state it allows for 2D heat map visualization. They explicitly dismiss standard physics-based control tasks like MuJoCo, cartpole, and mountain car. This narrow benchmark choice makes it impossible to assess whether the method scales to continuous state spaces or complex dynamics. Furthermore, the feature engineering in Objectworld is hand-crafted and relatively simple, which contrasts with the paper's claim of handling highly nonlinear reward structures. Expanding the evaluation to at least one continuous control benchmark or a standard IRL suite is necessary to validate the method's generalizability.

- Evaluation metrics and baselines lack statistical rigor: The primary metric is Expected Value Difference (EVD), which measures the performance gap between the recovered policy and the optimal policy. While useful, EVD alone cannot distinguish between reward recovery accuracy and policy optimization success. The paper omits standard reward recovery metrics like parameter error, cosine similarity, or KL divergence between the expert and recovered policies. Additionally, the comparison includes a baseline called DSIRL without a clear definition or citation. The results also lack statistical reporting, such as mean and standard deviation over multiple random seeds or confidence intervals. The authors should add parameter recovery metrics, clarify the DSIRL baseline, and report results over multiple independent runs with significance testing.

- Failure to address reward non-uniqueness and identifiability: The paper frames SIRL as a solution to the ill-posed nature of IRL by recovering a distribution over rewards. However, the robustness experiment in Section 4.2.3 shows that multiple distinct reward functions yield similarly low EVD values. This highlights that the method captures solution multiplicity rather than resolving the underlying ambiguity. The authors do not discuss how to select a single reward for deployment, nor do they analyze whether the learned GMM distribution reflects genuine epistemic uncertainty or merely flat regions in the reward landscape. A discussion on identifiability conditions and a practical strategy for reward selection or uncertainty quantification would strengthen the paper's claims.

- Verification that SIRL recovers MaxEnt as a special case: The manuscript claims to generalize IRL to a well-posed expectation optimization problem that builds upon the MaxEnt approach. The paper never demonstrates that the proposed formulation reduces to the standard MaxEnt objective when the reward weight distribution collapses to a point mass. Without this limit analysis, the claimed generalization remains unverified. Readers need to see that the MCEM updates simplify to the maximum likelihood weight estimation used in MaxEnt when the GMM variance approaches zero. Adding a theoretical derivation or a numerical experiment where the GMM is constrained to a single component would validate the generalization claim.

- Demonstration of reward transferability across different tasks: Despite repeated claims of transferability in the abstract and introduction, the experiments only evaluate performance on the exact Objectworld configuration used to generate the expert demonstrations. Transferability in IRL typically means using a learned reward function to guide policy learning in a perturbed environment or a completely new task layout. The current evaluation provides no evidence that the method actually transfers knowledge. The authors should include an experiment where the recovered reward is used to train a policy in a modified Objectworld grid or a different MDP with similar dynamics but different initial object placements.

- Guidance for selecting the number of GMM components: The proposed approach uses a Gaussian Mixture Model to approximate the reward weight distribution, yet the manuscript fixes the number of components $K$ to three without justification. Model selection is critical in mixture modeling because an underspecified $K$ will miss distinct reward modes, while an over-specified $K$ introduces unnecessary variance and computational overhead. The text lacks any discussion of how practitioners should choose $K$, whether through information criteria like BIC, cross-validation, or domain knowledge. Adding a section on model selection procedures or reporting performance across a range of $K$ values would make the method practically usable.

- Analysis of performance when the regular structure assumption fails: The method depends heavily on the assumption that the true reward function admits a regular structure as a linear or nonlinear combination of hand-crafted features. The experiments do not test what happens when this assumption is violated, such as when the expert policy depends on a feature outside the provided basis. A method that silently degrades under feature misspecification offers little practical value. The authors should include an ablation study where the true reward contains a component orthogonal to the feature basis, or where noise is added to the features. Reporting how the EVD and recovered distribution change under these conditions would clarify the method's limitations.

Recommendation: Major revision. The algorithmic derivation conflates latent variable sampling with parameter updates, breaking standard EM guarantees. The evaluation is confined to a narrow grid-world benchmark and lacks standard reward recovery metrics or statistical reporting. Addressing these methodological and experimental gaps is essential before the work can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Reformulate the two-stage update procedure to strictly adhere to the E-step/M-step decomposition of MCEM, or provide a rigorous derivation justifying the proposed variant.

- Expand the experimental evaluation to include at least one continuous control environment or a standard IRL benchmark suite, and clarify the DSIRL baseline.

- Add standard reward recovery metrics (parameter error, cosine similarity) and report mean/std over multiple random seeds with confidence intervals.

- Provide a theoretical discussion or proof regarding convergence conditions specific to the non-convex IRL landscape, rather than relying solely on general MCEM citations.

- Include a practical discussion on how to select a single reward from the learned distribution for deployment and analyze whether the distribution captures uncertainty or merely solution multiplicity.

comments.title: Ill-defined expectation and raw density maximization in Eq. (1)
comments.quote: $\Theta^{*}:=\arg\max_{\Theta}\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{O}\in\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}}\big{[}\int_{\mathcal{W}}f_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{W}|\Theta)d\mathcal{W}\big{]},$ (1)
comments.explanation: The expectation operator $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{O}\in\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}}$ is mathematically ill-posed because $\mathcal{C}^{E}_{\epsilon}$ is a discrete combinatorial set of subsets, not a probability space. Even if interpreted as a uniform average over all subsets satisfying the cardinality constraint, the objective maximizes the marginal density $f_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O}|\Theta)$ rather than its logarithm. Maximizing raw probability densities is statistically unsound and fails to account for the scale of the data, while averaging over all possible subsets discards the specific structure of the observed demonstrations. Add a precise probability measure to the expectation or reformulate the objective as the standard log-likelihood $\arg\max_{\Theta} \log f_{\mathcal{M}}(\zeta^{E}|\Theta)$ to establish a statistically valid learning criterion.

comments.title: Missing data conditioning in EM update for prior parameters
comments.quote: $\Theta_{2}^{t+1}$ $:=\arg\max_{\Theta_{2}}\mathbb{E}\Big{(}\log h_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{W}|\Theta_{2})\Big{|}\Theta^{t}\Big{)}.$ (5)
comments.explanation: Equation 5 omits conditioning on the observed data. In the EM algorithm, the M-step maximizes the expected complete-data log-likelihood $Q(\theta|\theta^t) = \mathbb{E}_{W|O,\theta^t}[\log f_M(O, W|\theta)]$. Since the complete data is $(O, W)$ and only $O$ is observed, the expectation must be taken with respect to the posterior $p(W|O, \theta^t)$. Equation 5 conditions only on $\theta^t$, which implies an expectation over the prior $h_M(W|\theta_2^t)$. This would prevent $\theta_2$ from adapting to the expert demonstrations. Rewrite Equation 5 as $\Theta_{2}^{t+1} := \arg\max_{\Theta_{2}}\mathbb{E}\Big{(}\log h_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{W}|\Theta_{2})\Big{|}\mathcal{C}_{c}^{E},\Theta^{t}\Big{)}$ because the EM M-step requires conditioning the expectation on the observed data to incorporate expert demonstrations.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of iterative gradient ascent update
comments.quote: $\mathcal{W}_{i}^{m\Theta^{t}}:=\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}}+\sum_{i=1}^{m}\lambda_{i}^{t}\cdot\nabla_{(\Theta_{1})_{i}}\log g_{\mathcal{M}_{i}^{t}}\big{(}\mathcal{O}_{i}^{t}|\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}},(\Theta_{1})_{i}\big{)},$
comments.explanation: Even after fixing the index collision, the update formula sums gradients evaluated at the initial point $\mathcal{W}_i^{\Theta^t}$ (or $(\Theta_1)_i$) rather than updating the parameter iteratively at each step. Standard gradient ascent for $m$ steps would follow $\theta^{(k+1)} = \theta^{(k)} + \lambda_k \nabla \log g(\theta^{(k)})$ for $k=1,\dots,m$. Rewrite the update formula as an iterative sequence $\theta^{(k+1)} := \theta^{(k)} + \lambda_k^t \nabla_{\theta} \log g_{\mathcal{M}_i^t}(\mathcal{O}_i^t | \mathcal{W}_i^{\Theta^t}, \theta^{(k)})$ for $k=1,\dots,m$ because the current summation incorrectly assumes gradients are evaluated at the initial point rather than updating the parameter at each step.

comments.title: Missing method-to-value mapping contradicts performance claims
comments.quote: In Figure 1, the EVD of the optimal values in the last row are 48.9, 31.1, 33.7 and 11.3 for four methods respectively, and the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively.
comments.explanation: The paragraph lists four EVD values but never explicitly assigns each number to MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, or DSIRL. Without a clear mapping, readers cannot verify the performance comparison. Furthermore, EVD measures error, so lower values indicate better recovery. If the values follow the order of mention (MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, DSIRL), then SIRL's EVD of 33.7 is actually worse than DeepMaxEnt's 31.1, which would contradict the claim that SIRL and DSIRL outperform the baselines. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly list the methods in the same order as the EVD values (e.g., 'The EVDs for MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, and DSIRL are 48.9, 31.1, 33.7, and 11.3, respectively') because this removes ambiguity and allows readers to verify the performance claim.

comments.title: Misuse of "well-posed" in problem formulation claim
comments.quote: In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem *stochastic inverse reinforcement learning* (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions.
comments.explanation: Hadamard well-posedness strictly requires existence, uniqueness, and continuous dependence on input data. Recovering a distribution over reward functions does not guarantee uniqueness, as the inferred distribution remains highly sensitive to the chosen feature basis and prior specification. The abstract's assertion that SIRL resolves the ill-posed nature of IRL by making it well-posed is mathematically inaccurate. The authors should replace "well-posed" with "distributional" or "probabilistic" and clarify how identifiability is bounded or addressed. Rewrite "In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem *stochastic inverse reinforcement learning* (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions." as "In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a distributional expectation optimization framework called stochastic inverse reinforcement learning (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions." because "well-posed" incorrectly implies uniqueness and stability that the method does not guarantee.

comments.title: Stochastic policy domain notation is formally incorrect
comments.quote: A policy $\pi:\mathcal{S}\rightarrow\mathcal{A}$ is deterministic or stochastic, where the deterministic one is written as $a=\pi(s)$, and the stochastic one is as a conditional distribution $\pi(a|s)$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\pi:\mathcal{S}\rightarrow\mathcal{A}$ strictly defines a deterministic mapping from states to actions. A stochastic policy cannot be represented as a function into $\mathcal{A}$; it must map states to probability distributions over actions, i.e., $\pi:\mathcal{S}\rightarrow\Delta(\mathcal{A})$ or be defined explicitly as a conditional probability $\pi(a|s)$. The current phrasing conflates the domain and codomain of deterministic and stochastic policies, which creates ambiguity when deriving policy gradients or value functions later. Rewrite 'A policy $\pi:\mathcal{S}\rightarrow\mathcal{A}$ is deterministic or stochastic, where the deterministic one is written as $a=\pi(s)$, and the stochastic one is as a conditional distribution $\pi(a|s)$.' as 'A deterministic policy is a mapping $\pi:\mathcal{S}\rightarrow\mathcal{A}$ written as $a=\pi(s)$, while a stochastic policy is a conditional distribution $\pi(a|s)$.' because the original notation incorrectly assigns the codomain $\mathcal{A}$ to stochastic policies.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in linear reward definition
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{R}(s,a):=\sum_{i}^{M}\alpha_{i}\cdot\phi_{i}(s,a),$

where $\phi_{i}:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}\longmapsto\mathbb{R}^{d}$ are a $d$-dimensional feature functions hand-selected by experts.
comments.explanation: The equation defines the reward as a sum over $i=1 \dots M$ of $\alpha_i \cdot \phi_i(s,a)$. However, the text specifies that each $\phi_i$ maps to $\mathbb{R}^d$. For the dot product $\alpha_i \cdot \phi_i(s,a)$ to yield a scalar reward, $\alpha_i$ must also be a $d$-dimensional vector, not a scalar weight. In standard linear IRL formulations, either the features $\phi_i$ are scalar-valued ($\mathbb{R}$) and $\alpha_i$ are scalar weights, or the features are concatenated into a single vector $\phi(s,a) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with a single weight vector $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^d$. The current notation conflates these two conventions. Rewrite "$\mathcal{R}(s,a):=\sum_{i}^{M}\alpha_{i}\cdot\phi_{i}(s,a),$ where $\phi_{i}:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}\longmapsto\mathbb{R}^{d}$ are a $d$-dimensional feature functions" as "$\mathcal{R}(s,a):=\sum_{i=1}^{M}\alpha_{i}\phi_{i}(s,a),$ where $\phi_{i}:\mathcal{S}\times\mathcal{A}\longmapsto\mathbb{R}$ are scalar feature functions and $\alpha_{i}\in\mathbb{R}$ are scalar weights" because this resolves the dimensional inconsistency and aligns with standard linear reward parameterizations in IRL.

comments.title: Misidentification of weights versus basis functions
comments.quote: In the following section, we propose an optimization problem whose solution is a probability distribution over weights of basis functions $\{\phi_{i}(s,a)\}_{i=1}$.
comments.explanation: The phrase "weights of basis functions $\{\phi_{i}(s,a)\}_{i=1}$" incorrectly labels the feature functions $\phi_i$ as the weights. The weights are the coefficients $\alpha_i$ introduced in the previous equation. Additionally, the index range is truncated to $i=1$ instead of $i=1 \dots M$. This should be corrected to explicitly refer to the distribution over the weight coefficients $\{\alpha_i\}_{i=1}^M$ associated with the basis functions $\{\phi_i(s,a)\}_{i=1}^M$. Rewrite "whose solution is a probability distribution over weights of basis functions $\{\phi_{i}(s,a)\}_{i=1}$." as "whose solution is a probability distribution over the weight coefficients $\{\alpha_i\}_{i=1}^M$ of the basis functions $\{\phi_i(s,a)\}_{i=1}^M$." because it correctly distinguishes between the fixed feature basis and the learnable reward parameters, and restores the proper summation index.

comments.title: Undefined notation and missing E-step posterior specification
comments.quote: $f_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{W}|\theta_{1},\theta_{2})=g_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O}|\mathcal{W},\theta_{1})\cdot h_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{W}|\theta_{2}).$ (2)
comments.explanation: The symbol $\mathcal{C}_{c}^{E}$ appears in Equation 4 without definition, while the observed data is denoted as $\mathcal{O}$ in Equations 1–3. Readers will wonder whether $\mathcal{C}_{c}^{E}$ represents the collected expert trajectories $\mathcal{O}$ or a different dataset. Additionally, the EM derivation skips the E-step entirely. The expectation in Equations 4 and 5 is taken over the latent variable $\mathcal{W}$, but the paper does not specify the posterior distribution $p(\mathcal{W}|\mathcal{O}, \theta^t)$ used to compute it. Add a definition stating $\mathcal{C}_{c}^{E} \equiv \mathcal{O}$ and explicitly specify the posterior $p(\mathcal{W}|\mathcal{O}, \theta^t) \propto g_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{O}|\mathcal{W},\theta_1^t)h_{\mathcal{M}}(\mathcal{W}|\theta_2^t)$ after Equation 2 to address the undefined notation and missing E-step derivation.

comments.title: Index collision in gradient ascent summation
comments.quote: $\mathcal{W}_{i}^{m\Theta^{t}}:=\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}}+\sum_{i=1}^{m}\lambda_{i}^{t}\cdot\nabla_{(\Theta_{1})_{i}}\log g_{\mathcal{M}_{i}^{t}}\big{(}\mathcal{O}_{i}^{t}|\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}},(\Theta_{1})_{i}\big{)},$
comments.explanation: The summation index $i$ is reused for the inner gradient ascent steps ($1$ to $m$), directly clashing with the outer Monte Carlo sample index $i$ ($1$ to $N_t$). This creates ambiguity in the gradient term $\nabla_{(\Theta_1)_i}$ and the learning rate $\lambda_i^t$, as it is unclear whether they refer to the sample index or the step index. Rewrite the summation as $\sum_{k=1}^{m}\lambda_{k}^{t}\cdot\nabla_{(\Theta_{1})_{i}}\log g_{\mathcal{M}_{i}^{t}}\big{(}\mathcal{O}_{i}^{t}|\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}},(\Theta_{1})_{i}\big{)}$ because changing the inner index to $k$ resolves the collision and correctly isolates the step-wise optimization from the Monte Carlo sampling loop.

comments.title: Conflation of optimization parameters and reward weights
comments.quote: Hence, the parameter $\Theta_{1}^{t+1}$ is represented as $\Theta_{1}^{t+1}:=\Big{(}\mathcal{W}_{1}^{m\Theta^{t}},\cdots,\mathcal{W}_{N_{t}}^{m\Theta^{t}}\Big{)}$.
comments.explanation: The text defines $(\Theta_1^{t+1})_i$ as the maximizer of the log-likelihood with respect to an abstract parameter $\theta$, but then immediately equates the updated parameter vector $\Theta_1^{t+1}$ to the updated reward weights $\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^t}$. The gradient update applies to $\mathcal{W}_i^{\Theta^t}$, yet the gradient is explicitly taken with respect to $(\Theta_1)_i$. Rewrite the final sentence as 'Hence, the parameter $\Theta_1^{t+1}$ is represented as $\Theta_1^{t+1} := ((\Theta_1^{t+1})_1, \dots, (\Theta_1^{t+1})_{N_t})$, where each coordinate $(\Theta_1^{t+1})_i$ corresponds to the updated reward weight $\mathcal{W}_i^{m\Theta^t}$, because the current text conflates the abstract optimization variable with the concrete reward weight without establishing the mapping.' This clarifies that $\Theta_1$ and $\mathcal{W}$ represent the same underlying quantity in this context.

comments.title: Unjustified convexity claim for IRL likelihood
comments.quote:
$(\Theta_{1}^{t+1})_{i}:=\arg\max_{\theta}\log g_{\mathcal{M}_{i}^{t}}\big{(}\mathcal{O}_{i}^{t}|\mathcal{W}_{i}^{\Theta^{t}},\theta\big{)},$

which is a convex formulation maximized by a gradient ascent method.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the log-likelihood $\log g_{\mathcal{M}_i^t}(\mathcal{O}_i^t | \mathcal{W}_i^{\Theta^t}, \theta)$ is convex in $\theta$. In stochastic IRL, the likelihood of observed trajectories with respect to reward parameters is typically non-convex due to the nested optimization required to compute state visitation frequencies or policy probabilities. Rewrite the clause as 'which is optimized via gradient ascent' because the log-likelihood in stochastic IRL is generally non-convex due to nested policy optimization, and asserting convexity without proof misleads readers about the optimization landscape.

comments.title: Misattribution of MCEM convergence citations
comments.quote: Additional requirement for the convergence property is a compact assumption over that parameter space. A comprehensive proof refers to *Chan and Ledolter (1995); Fort et al. (2003)*.
comments.explanation: The cited references do not establish the general MCEM convergence conditions mentioned. Chan & Ledolter (1995) focuses on time-series latent variable models, and Fort et al. (2003) addresses Stochastic Approximation EM (SA-EM), not standard MCEM. The conditions of parameter space compactness and Monte Carlo variance decay ($\sum 1/N_t < \infty$) are formally established in Liu & Wu (1998) and Caffo et al. (2005). The citation should be updated to reflect the correct theoretical foundation. Rewrite the sentence as: "A comprehensive proof of these convergence conditions can be found in Caffo et al. (2005) and Liu & Wu (1998)."

comments.title: Conflation of practical termination and theoretical convergence
comments.quote:
In practice, we require the Monte Carlo sample size satisfy the following inequality,

$\sum_{t}\frac{1}{N_{t}}<\infty.$
comments.explanation: The text presents $\sum_{t} 1/N_t < \infty$ as a "practical" requirement, but this is a theoretical asymptotic condition required for the consistency of MCEM estimators (ensuring Monte Carlo noise vanishes sufficiently fast). It is not a practical stopping rule or a constraint easily enforced in finite-sample implementations. The section mixes practical termination heuristics (Sec 3.2) with asymptotic convergence theory (Sec 3.3) without distinguishing their purposes. Add a clarifying sentence after the inequality: "This condition ensures asymptotic consistency by guaranteeing that Monte Carlo variance decays sufficiently fast, rather than serving as a practical stopping rule."

comments.title: Mismatch between reward notation in formula and textual description
comments.quote:
$\text{EVD}(\mathcal{W}):=\mathbb{E}\big{[}\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}\cdot R(s_{t},a_{t})\Big{|}\pi^{*}\big{]}-\mathbb{E}\big{[}\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^{t}\cdot R(s_{t},a_{t})\Big{|}\pi(\mathcal{W})\big{]},$

which is a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy $\pi^{*}$, given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution $\mathcal{D}(\mathcal{W}|\zeta^{E},\Theta^{*})$, where $\Theta^{*}$ is the best estimation parameter in our approach.
comments.explanation: The formula uses the identical reward function $R(s_t, a_t)$ for both the optimal and recovered policies, but the accompanying text specifies that the first term uses the "true reward" while the second uses rewards "sampled from our reward conditional probability distribution". This creates a direct contradiction between the equation and the explanation. If the metric evaluates the recovered policy's performance under the true reward (standard for EVD), both terms should use $R_{\text{true}}$. If it evaluates each policy under its corresponding reward, the equation must distinguish between $R_{\text{true}}$ and $R(\mathcal{W})$. Add explicit subscripts to $R$ in the equation to match the intended evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Contradiction between Algorithm 2 description and DSIRL architecture
comments.quote: Four algorithms for the evaluation includes MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, and DSIRL, where SIRL and DSIRL are implemented as Algorithm 2 in Appendix.
comments.explanation: This statement directly conflicts with the later description of DSIRL's architecture. The text explicitly notes that DSIRL uses a "3-layer fully-connected with the sigmoid function" neural network, whereas Algorithm 2 outlines the two-stage GMM-based update procedure for SIRL. Claiming both methods use Algorithm 2 is mathematically and procedurally inconsistent. Rewrite "where SIRL and DSIRL are implemented as Algorithm 2 in Appendix" to "where SIRL is implemented as Algorithm 2 in Appendix, and DSIRL follows the neural network baseline described in [Ref]" to resolve the contradiction and correctly attribute the methodologies.

comments.title: Ambiguous covariance metric definition
comments.quote: and the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively.
comments.explanation: The term covariance refers to a matrix, not a single scalar per method. The phrase limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively is mathematically imprecise. The authors likely mean the maximum diagonal element (variance), the trace, or the determinant of the covariance matrix. Additionally, limited up to suggests a bound rather than a measured statistic. Rewrite 'covariances of GMM model... are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36' as 'the maximum variance along any dimension for the SIRL and DSIRL GMMs are 5.53 and 1.36, respectively' because covariance is a matrix and the current phrasing is mathematically imprecise.

comments.title: Logical impossibility in diversity constraint
comments.quote:
^ {T})}.
$$

We also constrain that each drawn weight  $\mathcal{W} \sim \mathcal{D}(\mathcal{W}|\zeta^E, \Theta^*)$  in the solution set  $\mathcal{G}$  satisfies as follows,

$\| \mathcal{W} - \mathcal{W}'\|_{\mathcal{F}} > \delta$  and  $\operatorname {EVD}(\mathcal{W}) <   \epsilon$

where  $\mathcal{W}'$  represents any member in the solution set  $\mathcal{G}$ .  $\d
comments.explanation: The constraint requires the distance between a candidate weight $\mathcal{W}$ and any member $\mathcal{W}'$ in $\mathcal{G}$ to exceed $\delta$. If $\mathcal{W}$ is treated as a member of $\mathcal{G}$ during this check, then $\|\mathcal{W} - \mathcal{W}\|_F = 0$, which violates the condition for any $\delta > 0$. The authors must clarify that $\mathcal{W}'$ refers only to previously accepted members, or explicitly exclude the candidate from the set. Rewrite the condition to specify that $\mathcal{W}' \in \mathcal{G} \setminus \{\mathcal{W}\}$ or 'any previously accepted weight in $\mathcal{G}$' to make the algorithm executable.

comments.title: Vague performance claim without quantitative baseline
comments.quote: A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach.
comments.explanation: The claim that an EVD is small is meaningless without a reference scale or absolute value. The authors need to state the actual EVD value achieved with fewer than 200 demonstrations. Without this, readers cannot assess whether the performance is competitive or merely acceptable. Rewrite "A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach." as "A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields an EVD of 0.XX, which demonstrates the efficiency of the Monte Carlo mechanism." because the original claim lacks a quantitative baseline.

comments.title: Missing baseline conditions for hyperparameter sweeps
comments.quote: We verify three hyperparameters including the number of expert demonstrations in Figure 3, the trajectory length of expert demonstrations in Figure 4 and the portion size in representative trajectory class  $\mathcal{C}_c^E$  in Figure 5 on the objectworld.
comments.explanation: The text describes varying three hyperparameters but fails to specify the fixed values for the other two during each sweep. For instance, when varying the number of demonstrations in Figure 3, what trajectory length and portion size were held constant? Without reporting these baseline settings, the results cannot be reproduced or properly contextualized. Add "For each experiment, the unvaried hyperparameters were held at their default values: trajectory length = 32, portion size = 0.80." after "We verify three hyperparameters including the number of expert demonstrations in Figure 3, the trajectory length of expert demonstrations in Figure 4 and the portion size in representative trajectory class $\mathcal{C}_c^E$ in Figure 5 on the objectworld." to address the missing experimental conditions.

comments.title: Conclusion claims well-posedness and MCEM solution without formal support
comments.quote: The new problem is well-posed and we employ the method of MCEM to give the first succinct, robust, and transferable solution.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the proposed SIRL problem is well-posed and that the MCEM method yields a robust, transferable solution. However, the provided formal results section only contains standard definitions of MDPs and the classical IRL setup. It does not define the SIRL formulation, introduce the MCEM algorithm, or provide any mathematical proofs regarding well-posedness or convergence. Consequently, the theoretical claims in the conclusion are not grounded in the formal results presented. To align the discussion with the formal content, either include the formal definitions and proofs of well-posedness for SIRL and MCEM in the results section, or remove the strong theoretical claims from the conclusion and reframe them as empirical observations or future theoretical work.

comments.title: Incorrect scoping of EM algorithm steps
comments.quote:
For $i=1,\cdots,N_{t}$, we have

- Expectation Step: Compute responsibility $\gamma_{ij}$ for $m$-step reward weight $\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}$,

$\gamma_{ij}:=\frac{\alpha_{j}\cdot\mathcal{N}(\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}|\mu_{j},\Sigma_{j})}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}\alpha_{k}\cdot\mathcal{N}(\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}|\mu_{k},\Sigma_{k})}.$
- Maximization Step: Compute weighted mean $\mu_{j}$ and variance $\Sigma_{j}$ by,

$\mu_{j}:=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{t}}\gamma_{ij}\cdot\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{t}}\gamma_{ij}};\quad\alpha_{j}:=\frac{1}{N_{t}}\cdot\sum_{i=1}^{N_{t}}\gamma_{ij};\quad\Sigma_{j}:=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{t}}\gamma_{ij}\cdot(\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}-\mu_{j})\cdot(\mathcal{W}^{m\Theta^{t}}_{i}-\mu_{j})^{T}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{t}}\gamma_{ij}}.$
comments.explanation: The introductory phrase 'For $i=1,\cdots,N_{t}$, we have' incorrectly scopes the E-step and M-step as per-sample operations. In standard EM, the E-step computes responsibilities for all samples, and the M-step performs a global update over the entire dataset. The summation indices in the M-step formulas correctly reflect this, but the leading phrase creates a logical contradiction. Rewrite the introduction to 'The EM procedure consists of the following steps:' to accurately reflect the dataset-wide nature of the updates.

comments.title: Metric mismatch between Euclidean features and grid steps
comments.quote: Each dimension records the Euclidean distance from the state to objects.
comments.explanation: In a grid environment where movement is restricted to four cardinal directions and staying in place, the natural distance metric is Manhattan (L1) or shortest-path grid distance, not Euclidean (L2). Using Euclidean distance for features while the reward function and transition dynamics are defined in terms of steps creates a geometric mismatch that may degrade feature-reward alignment. Replace Euclidean distance with Manhattan distance or grid distance to maintain consistency with the environment's geometry and action space.

comments.title: Ambiguous specification of trajectory length
comments.quote: The length of each expert demonstration is 5-grid size trajectory length.
comments.explanation: The phrasing "5-grid size trajectory length" is unclear and does not follow standard RL terminology. It is not obvious whether this refers to a fixed horizon of 5 steps, a horizon of 50 steps (5 times the 10x10 grid dimension), or a different metric. Since trajectory length directly impacts the number of transitions observed and the discount factor's cumulative effect, precise values are necessary for reproducibility. Rewrite this to state the exact number of steps per episode (e.g., "a fixed horizon of 50 steps") to eliminate ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing initialization and empty set handling
comments.quote:
Input:  $\mathcal{D}(\mathcal{W}|\zeta^E,\Theta^*)$  , required solution set size  $N$  and preset thresholds  $\epsilon$  and  $\delta$

Output: Solution set  $\mathcal{G} \coloneqq \{\mathcal{W}_i\}_{i=1}^N$ .

while  $i < N$  do
comments.explanation: The pseudocode omits the initialization of the loop counter $i$ and the solution set $\mathcal{G}$. Standard algorithmic convention requires stating $i \leftarrow 0$ (or $1$) and $\mathcal{G} \leftarrow \emptyset$ before the loop. Furthermore, if $\mathcal{G}$ is empty, the inner loop over $\mathcal{W}' \in \mathcal{G}$ will not execute, making the distance check vacuously true. This means the first sampled $\mathcal{W}$ will be added unconditionally, which may be intended, but the lack of explicit handling for the empty set case creates ambiguity. Add explicit initialization and a conditional check for the empty set to clarify the first insertion step. Rewrite the algorithm preamble to include `$i \leftarrow 0$, $\mathcal{G} \leftarrow \emptyset$` before the while loop.

comments.title: Indexing mismatch between sampling counter and solution set
comments.quote:
Output: Solution set  $\mathcal{G} \coloneqq \{\mathcal{W}_i\}_{i=1}^N$ .

while  $i < N$  do
comments.explanation: The output notation $\{\mathcal{W}_i\}_{i=1}^N$ implies that the index $i$ corresponds to the position in the solution set. However, $i$ is used as the sampling iteration counter in the `while` loop. Since the algorithm conditionally appends to $\mathcal{G}$, the sampling counter $i$ will generally exceed the number of accepted solutions. This notation incorrectly suggests a one-to-one mapping between sampling steps and solution indices. Rewrite the output specification as $\mathcal{G} \coloneqq \{\mathcal{W}_j\}_{j=1}^{|\mathcal{G}|}$ or explicitly state that $i$ tracks sampling attempts while a separate counter tracks accepted solutions.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a stochastic generalization of inverse reinforcement learning (SIRL) and uses a Monte Carlo EM approach to estimate a distribution over reward functions. The core idea of recovering a distribution rather than a point estimate is well-motivated given the ill-posedness of IRL. However, the paper suffers from several critical issues: the problem formulation is vague and lacks theoretical rigor, the algorithm is described in a confusing and incomplete manner, the convergence claims are not substantiated, and the experimental evaluation is extremely limited (single benchmark, no statistical tests, weak baselines). These problems collectively undermine the paper's contribution and its suitability for a top-tier venue.

Overview issues:

- Problem formulation is ill-defined and lacks theoretical grounding: The SIRL problem in Equation (1) is stated as an expectation over a 'representative trajectory class' C^E_ε, but this object is never properly defined. The paper says O is a 'subset of expert demonstrations with cardinality at least ε·|ζ^E|', but it does not specify how O is constructed or why uniform sampling over such subsets is justified. The conditional joint density f_M(O,W|Θ) is introduced without any concrete form or connection to the underlying MDP. The claim that 'Problem 1 is well-posed and typically not intractable analytically' is unsubstantiated—well-posedness requires existence, uniqueness, and continuous dependence on data, none of which are discussed. Without a precise probabilistic model linking trajectories, weights, and parameters, the entire optimization problem is a formal placeholder rather than a rigorous framework.

- Algorithm description is incoherent and contains internal contradictions: The two-stage hierarchical method is presented as a variant of MCEM, but the derivation is confusing. In Section 3.1.2, the parameter Θ_1 is said to have N_t coordinates, each corresponding to a learning task M_i^t, and is updated via gradient ascent on a posterior. However, the update formula for W_i^{mΘ^t} uses a sum over i from 1 to m, which is ambiguous—the index i appears both as the task index and the gradient step counter. The definition of Θ_1^{t+1} as the collection of m-step reward weights contradicts the earlier statement that Θ_1 is a parameter of g_M. Moreover, the second stage (Section 3.1.3) fits a GMM to the m-step weights, but the connection to the original EM derivation in Equations (4)-(5) is never made clear. The paper claims convergence based on references (Chan & Ledolter 1995; Fort et al. 2003) but does not verify that the required conditions (e.g., compact parameter space, increasing sample size) are satisfied by the proposed algorithm.

- Theoretical claims are unsubstantiated: The abstract and introduction claim that SIRL is 'well-posed' and that the MCEM approach is 'theoretically convergent', but no proofs or rigorous arguments are provided. The convergence discussion in Section 3.3 merely states that the sample size should satisfy Σ 1/N_t < ∞ and that a compact parameter space is needed, referencing external papers. However, the algorithm described does not clearly specify how N_t is chosen (the experiments double it each iteration, but this is not justified theoretically). The claim that the method avoids 'secondary maxima' (i.e., local optima) due to Monte Carlo exploration is presented without analysis—Monte Carlo methods can also get trapped, and no guarantee of global optimality is given. For a paper that positions itself as making a theoretical contribution, the absence of any theorem, proposition, or formal convergence result is a major gap.

- Experimental evaluation is insufficient for the claims made: The experiments are conducted on a single environment (objectworld) with only four methods compared: MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, and DSIRL. No statistical significance tests are reported—the paper shows only point estimates or plots with standard error bars. The baselines do not include state-of-the-art IRL methods such as AIRL (Fu et al., 2017), GAIL (Ho & Ermon, 2016), or Bayesian IRL (Ramachandran & Amir, 2007), which are discussed in the introduction. The evaluation metric EVD is used, but the paper does not compare performance across multiple random seeds or demonstrate that the improvement over baselines is statistically meaningful. The hyperparameter experiments (Figures 3-5) show trends but lack error bars or confidence intervals. For a paper claiming 'considerable performance', the evidence is thin.

- Critical details are missing for reproducibility: The paper does not provide code, hyperparameter settings for baselines, or clear descriptions of the neural network architectures used (e.g., number of layers, hidden units, activation functions beyond sigmoid). The objectworld configuration (e.g., number of colors, placement of objects) is partially described, but the exact setup for generating expert demonstrations (e.g., how the optimal policy is computed) is not specified. The termination criteria for MCEM are mentioned but the values of δ_MCEM and ε_MCEM are not given. Without these details, the experiments cannot be reproduced or verified. Top-tier venues require reproducibility as a minimum standard.

- Scope and limitations are not discussed: The paper does not acknowledge the assumptions underlying its approach: that the reward function is a linear combination of known features, that the transition dynamics are known and deterministic (the objectworld has stochastic transitions but this is not discussed in the method), and that the expert demonstrations are generated from an optimal policy. The 'representative trajectory class' is introduced to handle suboptimality, but its effectiveness is not analyzed. The paper claims the solution is 'transferable' but provides no experiments on transfer learning. The computational cost of the MCEM procedure (which requires solving multiple MDPs per iteration) is not discussed. These omissions make it difficult to assess the practical value of the method.

- No worked example verifying the SIRL formulation: The paper defines SIRL as an expectation optimization over a 'representative trajectory class' but never computes Equation (1) for any concrete model. A natural check would be to specify a simple MDP (e.g., a 2-state, 2-action grid with known transition probabilities), choose a single feature φ(s), and set the reward to α·φ(s). Then compute the integral over W and the expectation over O explicitly to show that the optimum Θ* recovers the true α. Without such a worked example, the reader cannot verify that the formulation is coherent or that the MCEM algorithm actually solves it. Adding this computation in a short appendix section would resolve the gap.

- Missing comparison to standard IRL baselines: The experiments compare only MaxEnt and DeepMaxEnt, but the introduction discusses Bayesian IRL, AIRL, and GAIL as related work. The paper claims 'considerable performance' but does not benchmark against these state-of-the-art methods on objectworld. A fair comparison would include at least one of these baselines (e.g., AIRL or GAIL) using the same evaluation metric (EVD) and demonstration setup. Without this, the reader cannot assess whether the stochastic approach offers any practical advantage over existing methods. The authors should run these baselines on the same objectworld configuration and report EVD values with standard errors across multiple random seeds.

- No discussion of estimation or implementation of the method: The paper derives a theoretical quantity (the parameter Θ of the reward distribution) but provides almost no guidance on how to estimate it in practice. Key implementation details are missing: how is the representative trajectory class C^E_ε sampled? How is the conditional density g_M defined? The algorithm requires solving multiple MDPs per iteration, but the computational cost is never analyzed. For a method that claims practical relevance, the authors should discuss the curse of dimensionality (e.g., how the number of Monte Carlo samples N_t scales with state/action space size) and provide a concrete algorithm pseudocode with termination thresholds. Adding a 'Practical Implementation' subsection would address this.

Recommendation: Reject. The paper's core ideas are interesting but the presentation is too vague and incomplete to constitute a publishable contribution. The problem formulation lacks rigor, the algorithm description is confusing and internally inconsistent, the theoretical claims are unsubstantiated, and the experimental evaluation is minimal. A major revision would require a complete rewrite of the methodology, inclusion of formal theorems, and a much more thorough empirical study on multiple benchmarks with proper baselines and statistical testing.

Revision targets:

- Provide a precise probabilistic model for the SIRL problem, including the likelihood function and the role of the representative trajectory class, and prove that the optimization problem is well-posed under specific conditions.

- Rewrite the algorithm description in a clear, step-by-step manner, resolving the contradictions in the definition of Θ_1 and the gradient update, and specify how the MCEM iterations relate to the EM derivation.

- Include formal convergence guarantees (theorems with assumptions) for the proposed MCEM variant, or at least a rigorous argument showing that the conditions from Chan & Ledolter (1995) or Fort et al. (2003) are satisfied.

- Expand the experimental evaluation to at least 3-4 standard IRL benchmarks (e.g., Gridworld, Highway, continuous control tasks), compare against state-of-the-art baselines (AIRL, GAIL, BIRL), and report results with statistical significance tests over multiple random seeds.

- Provide complete implementation details (code, hyperparameters, architecture specifications) and discuss limitations, including assumptions about feature linearity, known dynamics, and computational cost.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of well-posedness
comments.quote: In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem *stochastic inverse reinforcement learning* (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts well-posedness without any justification. Hadamard well-posedness requires existence, uniqueness, and continuous dependence on data—none of which are argued. The problem formulation in Equation (1) is stated as an expectation over a 'representative trajectory class' that is never properly defined, and the conditional joint density f_M(O,W|Θ) is introduced without concrete form. Without establishing these conditions, the claim of well-posedness is unsubstantiated. Remove the word 'well-posed' unless a formal definition and proof are provided, or replace with a more modest claim such as 'a regularized expectation optimization formulation'.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of theoretical convergence
comments.quote: We adopt the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) method to estimate the parameter of the probability distribution as the first solution to the SIRL problem. The solution is *succinct, robust, and transferable* for a learning task and can generate alternative solutions to the IRL problem.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no theorem, proposition, or formal convergence result for the MCEM algorithm in the context of SIRL. The convergence discussion in Section 3.3 merely references external papers (Chan & Ledolter 1995; Fort et al. 2003) without verifying that the required conditions (e.g., compact parameter space, increasing sample size) are satisfied by the proposed algorithm. The claims that the solution is 'robust' and 'transferable' are also unsubstantiated by any theoretical analysis or experimental evidence (the experiments only test on a single environment). Remove or qualify these claims unless formal proofs and supporting experiments are provided.

comments.title: Undefined conditional joint density f_M
comments.quote:
